lessons.title: Writing and reading files
lessons.body: The `open()` function opens a file to write ('w') or read ('r'). Using `with open(...) as file:` guarantees the file is closed automatically at the end, even if something goes wrong in the middle.
lessons.example:
# Writes to a file (creates it if missing, overwrites if it exists)
with open('grades.txt', 'w') as file:
    file.write('Ana: 8.5\n')
    file.write('Bruno: 7.0\n')

# Reads the content back
with open('grades.txt', 'r') as file:
    content = file.read()

print(content)

lessons.title: Reading line by line
lessons.body: A text file can be gone through line by line with a `for`, or read all at once with `.readlines()`, which returns a list of lines. Each line includes the trailing `\n`, so `.strip()` is handy to clean that up.
lessons.example:
with open('grades.txt', 'r') as file:
    for line in file:
        print(line.strip())   # .strip() removes the trailing \n

with open('grades.txt', 'r') as file:
    lines = file.readlines()
    print(f'the file has {len(lines)} lines')

lessons.title: Standard library modules
lessons.body: Python already comes with ready-made modules for common tasks — `import` brings those functions into your code. `math` has mathematical functions, `random` generates random values, `datetime` works with dates.
lessons.example:
import math
print(math.sqrt(16))       # 4.0
print(math.ceil(4.2))      # 5 (rounds up)

import random
print(random.randint(1, 10))   # a random number between 1 and 10

from datetime import date
today = date.today()
print(today.year)

exercises.title: Save and read back
exercises.prompt: Write a function `save_and_read(text)` that writes `text` to the file 'output.txt' and returns the content read back from the same file.
hints: Open the file with 'w' to write, then with 'r' to read.
Use with open(...) as file: for each operation.

exercises.title: Count lines in a file
exercises.prompt: Write a function `count_lines(lines)` that takes a list of texts, writes each one as a line in the file 'list.txt' (ending with \n) and returns how many lines the file has, using .readlines().

exercises.title: Sum numbers from a file
exercises.prompt: Write a function `sum_file(numbers)` that writes each number (one per line) to the file 'numbers.txt', then reads the file back and returns the sum of all values as an integer.
hints: Write with str(number) + '\n' for each item.
When reading, use int(line.strip()) to convert each line back to a number.

exercises.title: Square root with math
exercises.prompt: Use the math module to write a function `square_root(n)` that returns the square root of n.

exercises.title: Round up
exercises.prompt: Use the math module to write a function `round_up(n)` that returns n rounded up (the smallest integer greater than or equal to n).

exercises.title: Random draw within a range
exercises.prompt: Use the random module to write a function `draw(minimum, maximum)` that returns a random integer between minimum and maximum (both inclusive).

tests.name: the result is always within range
tests.code:
v = draw(1, 5)
assert isinstance(v, int) and 1 <= v <= 5, f'got {v}'

hints: random.randint(minimum, maximum) draws an integer in the range, including both ends.

exercises.title: Current year
exercises.prompt: Use the datetime module to write a function `current_year()` that returns today's year.

tests.name: returns the current year
tests.code:
import datetime
assert current_year() == datetime.date.today().year, f'got {current_year()}'

hints: Access .year to get just the year.

exercises.title: Largest value read from a file
exercises.prompt: Write a function `largest_from_file(numbers)` that writes the numbers (one per line) to the file 'values.txt', reads them back and returns the largest value (without using max()).

tests.name: does not use max()
tests.code:
import inspect
assert 'max(' not in inspect.getsource(largest_from_file), 'solve it with a loop, without max()'

hints: Read the numbers back from the file and compare them one by one, keeping the largest.
Remember to convert each line read into an int.
